title: Removing white watermarks from polished wood
subtitle: Lift a cloudy water ring from a polished surface without stripping the finish
excerpt: How to clear the white ring left when a wet glass sits on a polished wood surface. Covers the gentle heat method, the toothpaste-and-cloth method, and the wax-and-buff finish that hides any residual mark.

glossaryTerms.definition: A white cloudy mark left in a polished surface (lacquer, shellac, or wax) by water trapped under a glass or vase. The water has not penetrated the wood; it sits in the finish itself.

A traditional French polish based on shellac flakes dissolved in alcohol. Soft, warm-coloured, and reversible by alcohol. The standard finish on Victorian and Edwardian furniture before modern lacquers arrived.

content.text: A
water ring

on a polished surface is not in the wood. It sits in the finish itself, where trapped water has clouded the film. Most rings clear with one of three simple methods. The wood does not need stripping or refinishing.
When to use this technique

Use these methods on a white or cloudy ring on a clean polished surface, where the finish is still smooth to the touch. A dark or black ring means the water has reached the wood and the fix needs a deeper repair. A flaking or peeling finish needs a touch-up of the finish itself before any ring repair is attempted.
Method 1, gentle heat

Place a cotton tea towel over the ring. Set a household iron to its lowest setting, no steam. Press the iron onto the towel for three seconds, lift, and check. Repeat with three-second pulses until the ring fades. Heat drives the trapped water out of the finish. Do not leave the iron in one place; the finish softens and prints with the towel weave.
Method 2, toothpaste and cloth

Squeeze a pea of plain white toothpaste (not gel, not whitening) onto a soft cotton cloth. Rub in small circles over the ring with light pressure. Toothpaste has a mild abrasive that cuts back the top layer of the finish. After 30 seconds, wipe clean with a damp cloth. Dry with a fresh cloth.
Method 3, mayonnaise overnight

Cover the ring with a small spoon of mayonnaise. Lay a sheet of cling film over the top. Leave overnight. The oil in the mayonnaise displaces the trapped water in the finish. Wipe clean in the morning with a soft cloth. This is the gentlest method and suits a delicate
shellac finish
where heat or abrasion would damage the surface.
Method 4, wax over a faint residual mark

If a faint ghost of the ring remains after one of the above methods, rub a small amount of clear furniture wax into the area with a soft cloth. Buff to a shine with a fresh cloth. The wax blends the finish back to even and adds a thin protective layer.
What it looks like when it is right
The ring is gone. The surrounding finish is unchanged. The table reflects light evenly across the repaired area and the surrounding wood.
Common mistakes
Iron set too hot. The finish softens, the towel prints into it, and the ring clears but is replaced by a textured patch. Always start at the lowest setting and pulse in three-second steps.
Gel toothpaste or whitening toothpaste. Both contain stronger abrasives or peroxides that damage the finish. Plain white paste only.
Treating a dark ring with the heat method. A dark ring means water has reached the wood; heat will not move it. Use a wood bleach or a sanded refinish for dark rings.
